variants: Write 3 things you'd buy online.
List 3 products you'd personally spend money on.
Name 3 things people buy online that you actually care about.
tasks.why: Starting with what you know makes everything easier.

variants: Pick one of your ideas and search it on Amazon.
Google one of your ideas and see what comes up.
Look up one of your ideas on TikTok Shop.
tasks.why: Seeing what already sells tells you if there's demand.

variants: Write down which idea felt most interesting.
Circle the one idea you'd actually want to talk about.
Pick your favorite idea and write why.
tasks.why: You need one direction before you can move forward.

variants: Search your product idea on Amazon and look at the top 5 results.
Find 5 products on Amazon related to your idea.
Look up your idea on Amazon and note the first 5 items you see.
tasks.why: Seeing what already sells confirms people want it.

variants: Check how many reviews the top Amazon products have.
Look at the star ratings on the top Amazon results.
Count the reviews on the best-selling version of your product.
tasks.why: Review counts show how crowded or open a space is.

variants: Write one sentence: does this idea seem doable?
Write yes or no — does this idea still feel right?
Jot down your gut reaction to what you saw.
tasks.why: Checking in with yourself keeps you honest.

variants: Search your product idea on TikTok.
Look up your product idea on Instagram.
Search your idea on YouTube and see what videos come up.
tasks.why: Social media shows what real people are excited about.

variants: Find one video about your product and watch the comments.
Read the comments on a popular post about your idea.
Look at what people are saying under a TikTok about your product.
tasks.why: Comments tell you what people actually want or hate.

variants: Write one problem people mentioned in the comments.
Note the most common complaint you saw.
Write down one thing people wished was different.
tasks.why: Problems are opportunities to make something better.

variants: Google '[your idea] alternatives' and read 3 results.
Search 'best [your product]' and scan what shows up.
Look up '[your idea] reviews' and skim a few.
tasks.why: Knowing what else exists helps you find your angle.

variants: Find one product that costs under $40 in your space.
Look for a product in your idea that's easy to ship.
Find a product related to your idea that's small and lightweight.
tasks.why: Affordable, lightweight products are easiest to start with.

variants: Write the name of one product you could actually sell.
Note one specific product that caught your eye.
Write down the most promising product you found today.
tasks.why: Getting specific moves you from ideas to action.

variants: Search your product on Etsy and see what's selling.
Look at eBay listings for your product idea.
Check Pinterest for your product idea.
tasks.why: Different platforms show different buyer types.

variants: Pick one listing and read the full description.
Find a top-selling listing and note what makes it good.
Read one product listing like you're the buyer.
tasks.why: Good listings tell you exactly what buyers care about.

variants: Write one word that describes who buys this product.
Describe the type of person who would buy this in one sentence.
Write down who this product is for.
tasks.why: Knowing your buyer is more important than knowing your product.

variants: Look up where to buy your product in bulk.
Search Alibaba or AliExpress for your product.
Search '[your product] wholesale' and see what comes up.
tasks.why: Knowing where to get it tells you if this is actually possible.

variants: Write down the cheapest price you found.
Note the rough cost to buy one unit.
Write down a price range you saw for buying in bulk.
tasks.why: You need to know costs before you can figure out pricing.

variants: Write what you'd charge a customer for this product.
Pick a price you'd sell it for.
Write a fair selling price based on what you saw selling.
tasks.why: A rough price check tells you if the numbers could work.

variants: Write your product idea in one sentence.
Describe what you want to sell in two lines.
Write a single sentence: what is the product and who is it for?
tasks.why: If you can't say it simply, it needs more clarity.

variants: Tell a friend or family member what you want to sell.
Explain your idea to someone out loud.
Text someone your product idea and ask what they think.
tasks.why: Real reactions tell you more than research.

variants: Write down their reaction.
Note the first thing they said.
Write whether they seemed interested or not.
tasks.why: Honest feedback early saves you time later.

variants: Look at 3 online stores that sell something similar to your idea.
Find 3 brands in your space and look at their websites.
Google '[your product type] store' and look at 3 sites.
tasks.why: Studying real stores gives you a clear picture of the space.

variants: Note one thing each store does well.
Pick one thing you like about each store.
Write one word to describe each store's vibe.
tasks.why: Spotting what works helps you borrow smart ideas.

variants: Write one thing those stores are missing.
Note a gap or problem you saw across those stores.
Write down what you'd do differently.
tasks.why: Gaps are your opportunity to stand out.

variants: Think of a name for your store.
Write 3 possible store names.
Brainstorm 3 names that fit your product idea.
tasks.why: A name gives your idea a real identity.

variants: Check if your favorite name is taken as a .com.
Search your top name on Shopify to see if it's available.
Google your favorite name and see if anyone else uses it.
tasks.why: Availability matters before you get too attached.

variants: Pick your top name and write it down.
Choose one name to move forward with.
Commit to one name for now — you can always change it.
tasks.why: Making a decision keeps you moving.

variants: Search your store name on Instagram.
Check if your store name is available on TikTok.
Look up your store name on social media.
tasks.why: Matching handles across platforms makes your brand easier to find.

variants: Create an Instagram account for your store.
Make a TikTok account with your store name.
Set up one social media account for your brand.
tasks.why: Claiming your name now means no one else can take it.

variants: Write a one-sentence bio for your profile.
Fill in the bio on your new account.
Add a simple description to your social profile.
tasks.why: A complete profile looks real from day one.

variants: Go to Shopify.com and start a free trial.
Sign up for a Shopify account today.
Create your Shopify store — just the basics.
tasks.why: Having an actual store URL makes everything feel real.

variants: Enter your store name when Shopify asks.
Set up the basic store info in Shopify.
Fill in your store name and country in Shopify.
tasks.why: Getting the basics in place is step one of the setup.

variants: Write down your Shopify store URL.
Note what your store address looks like.
Copy your new store URL somewhere safe.
tasks.why: You'll need this link often going forward.

variants: Pick a free theme in Shopify.
Browse Shopify themes and pick one you like.
Choose a simple Shopify theme and apply it.
tasks.why: Your theme is your store's look — pick one and move on.

variants: Change your store's colors to match your brand.
Update the main color in your theme to something you like.
Pick 2 colors for your store and apply them.
tasks.why: Colors make your store feel intentional.

variants: Upload a simple logo or write your store name in the header.
Add your store name to the top of your site.
Put your brand name in the header area.
tasks.why: A named header makes the store look like yours.

variants: Write a short homepage headline for your store.
Write one sentence that describes what your store sells.
Draft a tagline for your store in one line.
tasks.why: A clear headline tells visitors exactly what you offer.

variants: Add that headline to your Shopify homepage.
Update your homepage with the tagline you wrote.
Put your one-liner in the hero section of your store.
tasks.why: The homepage is the first thing buyers see.

variants: Preview your homepage and see how it looks.
Click preview and look at your store like a visitor.
Check your store in preview mode.
tasks.why: Seeing it as a visitor helps you spot what to fix.

variants: Order a sample of your product if you can.
Find where to buy one unit of your product to test it.
Look up how to get one sample of your product.
tasks.why: Touching the product before selling it prevents surprises.

variants: Take 3 photos of your product or a similar one.
Find 3 free stock photos that match your product.
Screenshot 3 images that represent your product.
tasks.why: Photos are the most important part of any product listing.

variants: Pick the best photo and save it somewhere.
Choose your favorite photo from today.
Save the one image that looks most like your product.
tasks.why: One great photo beats ten average ones.

variants: Add your first product to Shopify.
Create a new product listing in your Shopify store.
Click 'Add product' in Shopify and fill in the basics.
tasks.why: A live product is the foundation of your store.

variants: Write a short product title and description.
Name your product and write 2 sentences about it.
Add a title and a brief description to your product.
tasks.why: Clear product info helps buyers decide to buy.

variants: Set a price for your product.
Enter the selling price for your product.
Add a price to your product listing.
tasks.why: No price means no sale.

variants: Upload your product photo to the listing.
Add at least one image to your product page.
Put a photo on your product listing.
tasks.why: Products without photos almost never sell.

variants: Read your product description out loud.
Read what you wrote about your product and see if it makes sense.
Say your product description aloud like you're telling a friend.
tasks.why: If it sounds weird out loud, rewrite it.

variants: Fix one sentence in your product description.
Change one thing that sounded off when you read it.
Rewrite any part that felt clunky.
tasks.why: Small edits make a big difference in how buyers feel.

variants: Set up shipping in Shopify.
Add a shipping rate to your store.
Go to Shopify settings and set one shipping option.
tasks.why: Buyers need to know how much shipping costs before they buy.

variants: Add your payment method to Shopify.
Set up Shopify Payments so you can accept money.
Connect a payment option in your Shopify settings.
tasks.why: You can't get paid without a payment method set up.

variants: Buy your own product as a test to make sure checkout works.
Place a test order in your store.
Go through checkout on your own store and make sure it works.
tasks.why: If checkout is broken, no one can buy.

variants: Look at your store on your phone.
Open your store URL on a mobile device.
Check how your store looks on a phone screen.
tasks.why: Most buyers will see your store on their phone.

variants: Fix one thing that looks off on mobile.
Adjust anything that's hard to read on your phone.
Update one element that doesn't look right on mobile.
tasks.why: A bad mobile experience loses buyers fast.

variants: Write one sentence about what your store is missing.
Note the one thing your store still needs.
Write down the biggest gap in your store right now.
tasks.why: Knowing the next fix keeps you moving forward.

variants: Write 3 bullet points about why someone should buy your product.
List 3 reasons your product is worth buying.
Write 3 benefits of your product in plain words.
tasks.why: Benefits sell better than features.

variants: Add those bullet points to your product description.
Put those 3 benefits into your product listing.
Update your product page with the benefits you wrote.
tasks.why: Good product pages answer every buyer question.

variants: Preview your product page and read it top to bottom.
Look at your product page the way a buyer would.
Read your full product page from start to finish.
tasks.why: Fresh eyes catch things editing mode misses.

variants: Post about your store on your personal social media.
Share your store link with your followers.
Post that you're starting a store and share the link.
tasks.why: Your first traffic should come from people who already trust you.

variants: Text 3 friends and tell them you launched a store.
Message 3 people you know and share your store.
Tell 3 people you know about your new store.
tasks.why: Word of mouth is free and fast.

variants: Ask one friend to look at your store and give honest feedback.
Send your store link to someone and ask what they think.
Get one real person to look at your store today.
tasks.why: Real feedback is worth more than any tool.

variants: Read the feedback you got and write down any repeated points.
Look at what people said about your store.
Write 2 things people mentioned about your store.
tasks.why: Patterns in feedback show what to fix first.

variants: Fix the most common issue people mentioned.
Make one change based on the feedback you received.
Improve one thing on your store from what you heard.
tasks.why: Acting on feedback builds a better store faster.

variants: Write what your store looks like now vs. when you started.
Note how much your store has improved.
Write one sentence on how far your store has come.
tasks.why: Seeing progress keeps you motivated to continue.

variants: Look at 3 brands on Instagram that sell similar products.
Find 3 stores on social media in your space.
Search your product type on Instagram and find 3 accounts.
tasks.why: Seeing what works for others speeds up your own learning.

variants: Note what kind of posts get the most likes for those brands.
Look at which posts are getting engagement for those accounts.
Find the most liked post from each of those 3 accounts.
tasks.why: High-engagement posts show what your audience responds to.

variants: Write one post idea you could make for your store.
Come up with one thing you could post about your product.
Note one content idea inspired by what you saw.
tasks.why: One good idea is enough to start posting.

variants: Take a photo of your product or something related to it.
Find or take one good image for your social media.
Create or find a visual for your first product post.
tasks.why: Visual content stops people from scrolling past.

variants: Write a short caption for that photo.
Write 2 sentences to go with your product image.
Draft a simple caption for your product post.
tasks.why: A caption gives context and a reason to click.

variants: Post it.
Share the post on your social account.
Publish your first product post.
tasks.why: Done is better than perfect when you're just starting.

variants: Look at your Shopify analytics and see if anyone visited.
Check how many people looked at your store.
Open Shopify analytics and see your visitor count.
tasks.why: Knowing your traffic tells you what's working.

variants: Check which page people visited most.
See where visitors went in your store.
Look at which product got the most views.
tasks.why: Popular pages show what people are actually interested in.

variants: Write one thing you want to improve based on what you saw.
Note one thing your analytics showed that surprised you.
Write down one action you'll take based on your traffic data.
tasks.why: Data is only useful when you act on it.

variants: Search for a Facebook group related to your product.
Find a Reddit community where your buyers hang out.
Search for an online forum about your product type.
tasks.why: Communities are free places to learn about your buyers.

variants: Read 5 posts in that community.
Scroll through that group or forum for a few minutes.
Look at what people talk about in that community.
tasks.why: Listening to buyers tells you what words they use.

variants: Write one phrase buyers use to describe the problem your product solves.
Note how they describe what they want.
Write one phrase from the community you could use in your store copy.
tasks.why: Using their words in your store makes buyers feel understood.

variants: Update your product description with a phrase from your research.
Add one real buyer phrase to your product page.
Rewrite one sentence in your listing using the words real buyers use.
tasks.why: Copy that sounds like your buyer builds instant trust.

variants: Add a frequently asked question to your product page.
Write one Q&A about your product and add it to the listing.
Answer one common buyer question on your product page.
tasks.why: Answering questions before buyers ask removes doubt.

variants: Preview your product page and read the new copy.
Look at your updated product page as a buyer.
Read your product page one more time after the edits.
tasks.why: Reviewing changes ensures you didn't break anything.

variants: Post a second piece of content about your product.
Make another post about something your product helps with.
Post a behind-the-scenes photo or note about your store.
tasks.why: Consistency is what builds a real following over time.

variants: Reply to any comments on your posts.
Respond to anyone who liked or commented.
Engage with anyone who interacted with your posts.
tasks.why: Responding to comments builds loyalty with early followers.

variants: Write what tomorrow's post will be about.
Plan one post for tomorrow.
Jot down your next content idea.
tasks.why: Planning ahead makes it easier to stay consistent.

variants: Look up one supplier for your product on Alibaba.
Find one place to buy your product in larger quantities.
Search for a wholesale option for your product.
tasks.why: Knowing your supply options prepares you for real orders.

variants: Note the minimum order size for one supplier.
Write down what it would cost to order 10 units.
Check how many units you'd have to buy at once.
tasks.why: Understanding order minimums affects your planning.

variants: Write whether this feels like a real path forward.
Note if this supplier option seems workable.
Write one sentence on whether this supply option makes sense.
tasks.why: A quick gut check saves time on bad paths.

variants: Think of a second product you could add to your store.
Write one more product idea that fits your brand.
List one other thing your buyer might also want.
tasks.why: A second product gives buyers more reasons to buy.

variants: Search that second product idea on Amazon.
Look up the second idea and see if it sells.
Check if your second product idea has demand.
tasks.why: A quick check confirms if it's worth adding.

variants: Write yes or no — will you add this second product?
Decide if this second product is worth your time.
Note whether you'll pursue this second product.
tasks.why: Making a decision keeps you from spinning your wheels.

variants: Write what you've built in the last 30 days.
List everything you've done for your store this month.
Write a short summary of where your store stands.
tasks.why: Tracking progress gives you momentum to keep going.

variants: Write the one biggest thing your store still needs.
Name the one thing that would help your store the most.
Write what's missing from your store right now.
tasks.why: Knowing your biggest gap tells you where to focus next.

variants: Write one goal for the next 30 days.
Set one simple target for month two.
Write what you want to have done by day 60.
tasks.why: A clear goal for the next phase keeps you on track.

variants: Make a short video showing your product.
Film a 15-second clip of your product.
Record yourself holding or using your product.
tasks.why: Video gets more attention than photos on social media.

variants: Post that video on TikTok or Instagram Reels.
Upload your product video to social media.
Share your short video clip online.
tasks.why: Short video is currently the fastest way to reach new people.

variants: Write what you'd do differently in the next video.
Note one thing to improve for your next video.
Write one idea for a follow-up video.
tasks.why: Each video teaches you something for the next one.

variants: Look at the TikToks or Reels getting views in your space.
Find 3 viral videos related to your product type.
Search your product on TikTok and look at the top videos.
tasks.why: Popular videos show the format and style that works.

variants: Write down what those videos have in common.
Note the style, length, or hook of the popular videos.
Pick one thing every popular video did that yours didn't.
tasks.why: Patterns in popular content are worth copying.

variants: Plan one video using a format you saw work.
Write an idea for a video using what you learned.
Note the next video you'll make and how it'll look.
tasks.why: Learning from what already works saves you time.

variants: Make a second short video about your product.
Film another quick clip for social media.
Record one more video for your brand account.
tasks.why: Posting consistently builds an audience faster than one great video.

variants: Post it and add a relevant hashtag.
Share your video and add 3 hashtags.
Upload your video with a caption and a hashtag.
tasks.why: Hashtags help new people find your content.

variants: Check how your first video did — views, likes, comments.
Look at the stats on your first video post.
See how many people watched your first video.
tasks.why: Early stats tell you what to keep doing.

variants: Write a simple return policy for your store.
Add a return policy to your Shopify store.
Write 2 sentences about what happens if a buyer wants a refund.
tasks.why: A clear return policy builds buyer trust.

variants: Add the return policy to your store's footer.
Create a policy page in Shopify.
Put your return policy somewhere buyers can find it.
tasks.why: Buyers look for return policies before they buy.

variants: Write a short shipping time estimate for your store.
Note how long it takes to ship your product.
Add an estimated delivery time to your product page.
tasks.why: Buyers want to know when their order will arrive.

variants: Find one person on social media with a small following who reviews products.
Search for a micro-influencer in your product space.
Find someone on TikTok or Instagram who posts about products like yours.
tasks.why: Small creators often say yes to free products for a post.

variants: Look at their content and see if their audience fits your buyer.
Check if their followers are the kind of people who'd buy your product.
See if their vibe matches your brand.
tasks.why: A bad fit gets you followers who won't buy.

variants: Write a 2-sentence pitch you could send them.
Draft a short message you'd send to that creator.
Write what you'd say if you reached out to them.
tasks.why: Having a draft ready makes it easier to actually send it.

variants: Send your pitch to one creator.
DM one small creator you found yesterday.
Reach out to one influencer about your product.
tasks.why: Sending the message is the only way to get a yes.

variants: Find a second creator to reach out to.
Look for another small account to contact.
Find one more person to pitch your product to.
tasks.why: Reaching out to multiple people increases your chances.

variants: Write what you'll say to the second creator.
Draft a message for the second creator.
Note how you'll tweak your pitch for the next outreach.
tasks.why: Adjusting your message each time makes it more effective.

variants: Post a third short video for your brand.
Make another quick video about your product.
Film one more video today.
tasks.why: Three videos in gives you enough to see what sticks.

variants: Compare views across your 3 videos.
Look at which of your videos got the most attention.
Check the stats on all 3 videos side by side.
tasks.why: Comparing your own content shows you what to keep doing.

variants: Write what your best video did differently.
Note why your top video outperformed the others.
Write one thing your best video had that the others didn't.
tasks.why: Double down on what works.

variants: Write 3 ideas for your next product photo.
Think of 3 ways to show your product visually.
Brainstorm 3 creative ways to photograph your product.
tasks.why: Better photos directly increase how many people click to buy.

variants: Take one better photo of your product.
Photograph your product in better lighting.
Shoot a new product photo with a cleaner background.
tasks.why: The best listing photo is the one that gets clicked.

variants: Update your product listing with the new photo.
Swap out your old product photo with the new one.
Replace your product image with the improved version.
tasks.why: Better photos in your listing can change your results immediately.

variants: Search '[your product] + gift' on TikTok.
Look up gift-related content for your product type.
See if anyone buys your product as a gift.
tasks.why: Gift buyers are often easier to sell to than direct buyers.

variants: Write one sentence about why your product makes a good gift.
Note who might buy your product as a gift and why.
Add a gift angle to your product description.
tasks.why: Positioning as a gift opens a whole new group of buyers.

variants: Post a social media idea using the gift angle.
Write a caption about your product as a gift.
Plan one post using the gift framing.
tasks.why: Gift-framed content tends to perform well year-round.

variants: Look at your store analytics for the past week.
Check your Shopify traffic for the last 7 days.
Review your store stats from this week.
tasks.why: Weekly check-ins show if things are trending up or down.

variants: Find the day with the most visitors this week.
See which day had the most traffic.
Look at your busiest day this week and why.
tasks.why: Knowing your best days tells you when to post.

variants: Write one thing you'll do more of next week.
Plan one change to make next week based on your data.
Note the one thing that drove the most traffic.
tasks.why: Acting on data is how you grow intentionally.

variants: Write a short story about why you started your store.
Write 2 sentences about what made you want to sell this product.
Jot down your personal reason for starting this brand.
tasks.why: Your story makes buyers care about buying from you.

variants: Post that story on social media.
Share why you started your store in a post.
Turn your story into a short caption and post it.
tasks.why: Personal posts often get more engagement than product posts.

variants: Add your story to your store's About page.
Put a version of your story on the About section of your site.
Update your about page with your personal reason for starting.
tasks.why: An About page with a story builds trust with first-time visitors.

variants: Look at your product from a buyer's eyes for 2 minutes.
Visit your product page as if you've never seen it before.
Click through your store pretending to be a first-time visitor.
tasks.why: Fresh perspective reveals problems you stopped seeing.

variants: Fix the first thing that feels off or unclear.
Update one thing that didn't look right as a visitor.
Improve one part of your store based on what you noticed.
tasks.why: One fix at a time keeps improving the buying experience.

variants: Write the next thing you'd fix after that.
Note the second issue you spotted.
Write what you'd work on if you had more time today.
tasks.why: Keeping a list means you never lose good ideas.

variants: Find 3 hashtags your buyers use.

Look at what hashtags appear on popular posts in your space.

variants: Make a video using those hashtags.
Post something with all 3 hashtags you found.
Create a post and add your research hashtags to it.
tasks.why: Hashtags are free reach — use them every time.

variants: Check the reach of that post after a few hours.
Look at how many views you got after posting.
See how the hashtagged post performed.
tasks.why: Tracking results helps you learn what brings in more viewers.

variants: Write 3 questions a buyer might have about your product.

List 3 doubts a first-time buyer might have.

variants: Answer those 3 questions and add them to your product page.
Write short answers to each question and put them on your listing.
Add a Q&A section to your product page.
tasks.why: More answered questions means fewer reasons not to buy.

variants: Read the whole product page one more time.
Preview your product page after the changes.
Check your listing top to bottom.
tasks.why: Every review pass makes the page a little better.

variants: Write a summary of what your store looks like at day 45.

Write down everything your store has at this point.
tasks.why: Knowing what's done helps you see what's missing.

variants: Write what your store still needs to feel complete.

Name the biggest thing your store is missing.
tasks.why: A clear gap list keeps you focused.

variants: Write your one goal for the next 2 weeks.
Set one thing you want to achieve by day 60.
Write a simple target for the next phase.
tasks.why: Short goals are easier to stick to than long ones.

variants: Make a TikTok showing how your product works.

Record a 'how to use this' video for your product.
tasks.why: Demo videos are the most convincing content you can make.

variants: Add your store link to your TikTok or Instagram bio.

Check that your social bio links to your store.
tasks.why: No link in bio means views don't turn into sales.

variants: Post the demo video.
Upload the how-to video to social media.
Share your product demo online.

variants: Look up how to run a Facebook or Instagram ad.

Watch a short video on how Meta ads work.
tasks.why: Understanding ads before you run them saves wasted money.

variants: Open Meta Ads Manager and look around.
Set up a Meta Ads account if you don't have one.
Log into Facebook Business Manager and explore it.
tasks.why: Getting familiar with the tool removes the fear of it.

variants: Write down one thing you learned about ads today.
Note the most useful thing you read or watched.
Write one new thing you understand about running ads.
tasks.why: Writing it down makes it stick.

variants: Find your best-performing video or post so far.
Look back at all your content and pick the best one.
Check which post got the most engagement.

variants: Boost that post with $5 as a test.
Run a small boost on your best post.
Put $5 behind your top-performing content.
tasks.why: A tiny test tells you if paid reach is worth it.

variants: Write down what you're hoping to see from the test.

tasks.why: Having a goal makes it easier to evaluate the result.

variants: Check how your boosted post is doing.

See how many people your boost reached.
tasks.why: Early results tell you if the approach is worth continuing.

variants: Check if anyone visited your store from that post.
